title: Session display Phase 3 — Detach (detachable windows)
body:
## Requirement

**Session display Phase 3 — Detach: detachable session windows.** Move a session into its own OS window (draggable to a 2nd monitor), session keeps running; reattach back into the main window.

- **Legacy stays the default** (non-breaking); detach is an **action**, not a third exclusive mode. Controlled via setting `global.sessionDisplayMode`.
- Builds on **Phase 1 tabs (#6)** + **Phase 2 settings window (#7)** (both done) — uses the multi-window pattern proven there.
- Central change: route `terminal-data` per session to the owning window (sidebar/attention events stay in the main window).

Detailed design and implementation plan → comment.

title: Terminal: working-status line renders stacked/duplicated (rare) — distinct from #85 garble
body:
## Requirement

The Claude working-status line occasionally renders **stacked/duplicated** — several
successive status frames sit on top of each other (e.g. three `Hashing…` lines with
*different* content: `22 24`, `16s ↓401`, `11s ↓138 · thought for 5s`) instead of the
single line overwriting in place. A resize heals it. **Occurs rarely.** Seen on build
`main @ 7e473e7` (which already carries the DPR-refit fix), WebGL off (DOM renderer).

This is **distinct from #85** (that was the horizontal char-drift *garble* = xterm.js#6015,
addressed by the DPR-refit). This is **vertical duplication** — old status rows not cleared
when the TUI rewrites the status line — a different mechanism.

**Candidate:** xterm.js#5801 (OPEN) — "ED2 (`\x1b[2J`) inside a DEC 2026 synchronized update
block resets viewportY, causing scroll yank during streaming redraws." Matches stacked status
frames during sync redraws. To be confirmed.

**Expected:** a single, in-place-updating status line; no stacking.

## Diagnosis (in progress)

Temporary probe added on a debug branch (never merged): **F5** = pure `terminal.refresh(0, rows-1)`
(render repaint), **F8** = `safeFit` (geometry re-fit) on the focused session. Press each on the
stacked ghost:
- F5 clears it → dirty-row render-ghost → a refresh-heal fixes it.
- F5 fails, F8/resize clears it → geometry/re-fit class.
- Neither → buffer/sync-viewport (xterm.js#5801) → upstream / deeper.

Keep open until the mechanism is identified.

title: WebGL context loss leaves a stale fit (no re-fit, no repaint)
body:
`WebglAddon.onContextLoss` (`public/terminal-manager.js`) disposes the addon and marks the DOM renderer as suggested, but never re-fits the terminal.

xterm silently continues on its DOM renderer, whose cell metrics differ from WebGL's (xterm.js#6015). Every other renderer switch compensates: `loadTerminalWebgl()` calls `safeFit()` after loading, and `showSession()` re-fits on the next frame when it restores a context suspended by the grid's off-screen handling. The loss path does neither.

Result: a visible terminal that loses its GL context keeps a fit computed for WebGL cell metrics while rendering with DOM metrics — the bottom row can clip, the same failure mode #81 traced to renderer swaps under fractional display scaling. It self-heals only on the next tab switch, when `showSession()` reloads WebGL and re-fits.

Raising the GL-context budget to 32 narrows the window but does not close it: driver-side losses still occur.

### Acceptance
- `onContextLoss` re-fits the terminal and forces a full repaint.
- The re-fit is deferred by one frame — cell metrics are reported only after the renderer swap has painted, matching the existing restore path.
- No re-fit is attempted on a terminal destroyed between the loss and the frame.

Split out of #81 (found while re-reviewing its WebGL invariants).

title: Onboarding: first-time-launch welcome screen
body:
Show a welcome screen on the very first launch to orient a new user, then never again unless reset.

## Requirement

- On first launch (fresh install — no global settings yet), show a welcome overlay: brief intro to the app + primary actions.
- **Actions**: "Import settings" (ties into settings import — migrate from another machine) and "Open settings" (first-time configuration), plus a Dismiss/close.
- Dismissing persists a flag so it never shows again (store `welcomeDismissed` in the global settings blob via `merge-setting`, so it rides existing persistence + export/import).
- Style matches existing dialogs (`new-session-overlay` pattern). UI text English.

Depends on the settings export/import feature for the "Import settings" action.

Plan/design in comments.

title: Backends: declare what each CLI can actually do (configFields is nearly empty for Pi, Hermes, Codex)
body:
A backend descriptor already carries the "what can this CLI do" contract: `configFields`. The Settings page and the Configure dialog are **generated** from it. It is just barely filled in:

| Backend | declares today | what the CLI actually offers (from its own `--help`) |
|---|---|---|
| Claude | 9 options | matches |
| Codex | 3 — model, approval, sandbox | plus `-c key=value` (arbitrary config override), its **own** `--profile`, `--oss`, `--local-provider`, `--image` |
| Pi | **1** — model | `--provider`, `--api-key`, `--system-prompt`, `--append-system-prompt`, `--thinking`, `--tools` / `--exclude-tools` / `--no-tools`, `--mode`, `--extension`, `--models` |
| Hermes | **1** — model | `--provider`, `-t TOOLSETS`, `--skills`, `--safe-mode`, `--yolo`, `--worktree`, `--tui`/`--cli` |

So Pi and Hermes are, in practice, **not configurable at all** from Switchboard — you get a model box and nothing else, while the CLI underneath takes a dozen meaningful switches.

This also corrects a claim made during the multi-LLM work: that Hermes has nothing worth configuring because it self-authenticates and we inject no env. True for **env** — and irrelevant, because Hermes takes its configuration as **flags**.

## Requirement

- Per backend, work out from its **real `--help`** (not from published docs — those were wrong three times already, see `docs/backend-formats.md`) which options are worth exposing, and declare them in that backend's `configFields`.
- Only options that are **meaningful and safe to set per session**. A flag that would break Switchboard's own contract (session storage location, output mode, non-interactive mode) does not belong in the list.
- `buildLaunch` must translate every declared field into the correct argv/env for that CLI, and nothing else must change: an option nobody sets must produce the byte-identical command line it produces today.
- Record what was found per backend in `docs/backend-formats.md` (that is where format/CLI reality lives).

Prerequisite for #161 (templates carry a backend) — a template is a saved set of exactly these fields.

title: Templates: choose the backend a template runs on (today every profile is silently Claude)
body:
A "profile" today is hard-wired to Claude at three levels: no field in the editor UI, no field in the persisted shape (`profiles.js` `validateProfile` whitelists `{id, name, env, icon}` and drops everything else), and `profileToDescriptor` (`backends/index.js:61-76`) fetches `registry.get('claude')` and delegates `buildLaunch` / `discoverSessions` / `parseSession` / `watchTargets` to it.

The editor never says so. Its title is *"New backend profile"*; the word "Claude" does not appear in the dialog at all. You pick a name, an icon and env vars, and are left to infer the binding from the `ANTHROPIC_*` variable names. Worse, the icon grid offers the `codex`, `hermes` and `pi` icons — so you can put a Codex badge on a session that runs Claude.

## Requirement

- **A template names its backend.** It is a saved set of values for that backend's own `configFields` (#160), plus an optional env bundle. "Codex with model X and this sandbox setting" must be expressible; so must today's "Claude against DeepSeek".
- `profileToDescriptor` generalises: the base backend comes from the template, not from a hardcoded `'claude'`. Its store, parser and watcher are the base backend's — a template never has a store of its own.
- **Guard the base.** The current code dereferences `claude.buildLaunch` unguarded; a template whose base is missing or unregistered must fail with a clear reason, not throw.
- **Templates/presets are per backend.** The shipped presets (DeepSeek, GLM, OpenRouter) write `ANTHROPIC_*` env vars — they are presets *for the Claude base*, and the list must be scoped to the chosen base rather than offered globally.
- **Two homes, one setting — avoid it.** Launch options already cascade through `backendDefaults.<id>` (global → project). A template now also carries option values. Rule: a template's explicitly set options win for that launch; options it does not set fall through to the base backend's normal cascade. (#149 was exactly this trap once already.)
- **Icon** must not be able to claim another backend's identity.
- **Migration:** existing profiles carry no base — they are Claude's, and must keep launching after the update.
- Id rules stay: a template id may not collide with a built-in backend id (`RESERVED_IDS`).

Depends on #160. Related: #162 (a disabled backend takes its templates with it).

title: Claude must be disableable like any other backend (the lock is renderer-only, and the model would not survive it)
body:
Every backend has an enable toggle. Claude's is rendered `checked disabled` with the tooltip *"Built-in — always enabled"* — a single line of renderer code (`public/backends-panel.js:630`, `const locked = b.id === 'claude'`).

Two things are wrong with that.

**The rule exists nowhere but the UI.** `isEnabled()` (`backends/index.js:49-53`) has no Claude carve-out: `id === 'claude'` is only the default when nothing is stored, and a stored `backendEnabled.claude = false` wins. `test/backends.test.js:215` even asserts that a disabled Claude cannot spawn. No spec text says Claude is exempt — §5.8 defines the toggle for every `ready` backend and makes no exception. So the invariant is enforced in the one layer that cannot enforce anything: a hand-edited settings blob, or a **settings import**, can set the flag and the app will honour it.

**And the model would not survive it.** With `backendEnabled.claude = false`, Switchboard would half-break rather than cleanly drop Claude:

- `main.js:3663` — every session with no recorded backend falls back to `'claude'`, then `main.js:3669` refuses the spawn. That is **every legacy session** (rows predating multi-LLM carry no `backendId`) and every plain new session.
- `getDefaultLaunchTarget()` (`backends/index.js:163`) returns `'claude'` regardless — a default target that `isLaunchable()` rejects. Same fallback in `public/backend-registry.js:18` and `backends-panel.js:761`.
- Meanwhile the `claude` binary **keeps running anyway**: the scheduler builds `claude …` with no gate check (`main.js:4614`), and Claude's store keeps being scanned — `refreshBackendSessions` deliberately skips Claude (`session-cache.js:537`, "shares Claude's store") while the unconditional `PROJECTS_DIR` walk never consults `backendEnabled`.

## Requirement

- **Claude is a backend like any other.** The enable gate moves into the model (a settings blob or an import must never be able to produce a state the app cannot handle), and the renderer stops special-casing it.
- **Disable is not delete** (§5.8, unchanged): a disabled Claude keeps its sessions visible, searchable and viewable. They are simply not launchable and its store is no longer scanned.
- **Resuming a session whose backend is disabled** must refuse with a clear, named reason — including every legacy session, which resolves to Claude by definition.
- **The default launch target** must resolve to an actually launchable backend, never to a hardcoded `'claude'`.
- **The scheduler** must respect the gate: scheduled runs are Claude-only today, so with Claude disabled they must either target another backend or refuse — not silently spawn a disabled binary.
- **A disabled backend takes its templates with it** (decided: strict uniform treatment). A template runs its base backend's binary, so disabling the base disables the template. Note the consequence, deliberately accepted: a DeepSeek/GLM template runs the *claude* binary against another endpoint, so it too stops working while Claude is disabled.

Depends on #161 for the template half. Related: #160.

title: Remote repo over SSH (treat like local)
body:
## Requirement

**Remote repo over SSH (treat like local)** _(Source: supacode)_ — open a repo/project on a remote host via SSH and manage it in Switchboard like a local one: session scan, terminal (PTY over SSH), file/diff access. **Large** — needs its own detailed plan (transport, auth via Bitwarden SSH agent, remote `~/.claude/projects` scan, PTY bridging, watcher replacement). Clarify scope/architecture before implementation.

title: Detect subagent spawn via .meta.json (not only .jsonl)
body:
## Requirement

**Detect subagent spawn via `.meta.json` (not only `.jsonl`)** — when spawning a task subagent, Claude Code immediately writes `<parent>/subagents/agent-<id>.meta.json` (`agentType`, `description`, `toolUseId`, `spawnDepth`), but the `.jsonl` transcript only once the agent produces output. A spawned agent that did not (yet) run to producing output (queued, parent turn ends/is interrupted) has **only** `meta.json` → `detectSubagentTransitions`/`enumerateSessionFiles` (which only scan `subagents/*.jsonl`) never see it (observed 2026-07-02: session `876c2b28`, "Feldtexte W6" agents — meta identical to runnable W5 agents, but without jsonl; parent last at 22:26, spawns at 22:28). **No special background-agents format, no data loss — pure visibility.** Fix: also scan `subagents/*.meta.json` → placeholder subagent row (name from `description`, status "spawned/empty/aborted") as soon as the meta is there; upgrade to a full row when the `.jsonl` arrives. Files: `read-session-file.js` (`enumerateSessionFiles`), `session-transitions.js`, `session-cache.js`, subagent rendering in `public/sidebar.js`.

title: Per-session API key override
body:
## Requirement

**Per-session API key override** _(split off from #47)_ — optional password field in the session dialogs that injects `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` **only transiently** per session into the PTY `env` (`open-terminal`, `main.js`) — **never** in the command string or the settings DB. Persistence only via `saved_variables` `secret` (`safeStorage`). Picker layer from `brianstanley` @ `fb81d18`. **Net-new** (we are OAuth/Claude-only) — before implementing, clarify whether it's even wanted.

title: Make the terminal LRU cap configurable
body:
The renderer keeps at most 12 live xterm instances (`TERMINAL_LRU_CAP` in `public/terminal-manager.js`); the least-recently-shown closed session beyond the cap is destroyed (soft cap: running and active sessions are never evicted).

Since WebGL is the default renderer, the cap also serves as the GL-context budget (Chromium allows ~16 per process; a lost context falls back to the DOM renderer).

Expose the cap as a setting for users with many parallel sessions and enough RAM. Constraints: keep 12 as the default; values above ~14 should hint that terminals beyond the GL budget silently fall back to the DOM renderer.

Deferred from #81 (was rejected there as "defer until the cap is hit").

title: Session tags: add a discoverable entry point (can't add the first tag)
body:
## Problem

Session tags exist (display chips via `decorateSessionItem`, editor via `editTags` in `bookmarks-tags.js`) but are effectively unusable: the **only** way to open the tag editor is clicking an **existing** tag chip. A session with no tags shows no chip, so there is **no way to add the first tag** from the UI — no button, no context-menu entry.

## Requirement

Add a discoverable entry point to edit a session's tags, e.g. an "Edit tags…" item in the session right-click/context menu (and/or a small add-tag affordance). Opening it should call the existing `editTags(session)` dialog.

title: Evaluate: multi-sidebar panes
body: Evaluate porting aaaron `feat/multi-sidebar-panes` — multiple sidebar panes. Source: aaaronmiller/switchboard @ feat/multi-sidebar-panes.

title: Evaluate: local Whisper voice input
body: Evaluate porting ivandobsky `feat/local-whisper-voice` — local Whisper voice control/input. Source: ivandobskygithub/switchboard @ feat/local-whisper-voice.

title: Evaluate: brianstanley cursor-ui-refresh
body: Evaluate brianstanley `cursor-ui-refresh` — UI refresh. Source: brianstanley/switchboard @ cursor-ui-refresh. We already ported brianstanley's Variables + preview panel; check what's new/portable.

title: Optional second tray icon showing Claude usage (live percentage)
body:
Add an optional **second system-tray icon** that shows current Claude usage, enabled via Settings (off by default).

## Requirement
- A live-rendered tray icon showing the current usage percentage, styled like a usage monitor: a **colored progress ring** or a **badge** with the number baked into the icon, color-coded by the existing warn/critical thresholds (green → amber → red).
- **Tooltip** with the details we already show in the status bar (5h / 7d, extra usage, reset time).
- Small **context menu**: show/hide the window, open Stats, refresh, quit.
- **Cross-platform**: macOS, Windows, Linux.
- **Reuse the usage data the app already has** — the same values the status bar renders. **No separate polling loop / no extra network fetch** for the tray; it updates whenever the existing status-bar usage updates.
- Enabled + styled via a **Settings** toggle (icon style ring/badge). When off, the second tray is not created; toggling off destroys it.

Reference behaviour: a small pystray + Pillow usage-monitor tray that draws a ring/badge with the percentage and threshold colors.

title: Terminal: evaluate VSCode-style shell integration (OSC 633) for plain terminals
body:
Idea from the VSCode draw-path comparison (#87/#114 context). VSCode's shell integration injects a shell profile hook that emits OSC 633 sequences (command start / command end / exit code / cwd), enabling command tracking, "run recent command", per-command scrollback navigation, and precise prompt detection.

**Requirement:** evaluate whether a slim variant is worth it for our **plain terminals** (not Claude sessions — the CLI is a fullscreen TUI where command tracking is meaningless): e.g. exit-code badges, jump-to-previous-command in scrollback, or more reliable "shell is idle" detection. Likely low value for this app's core use case; document a decision either way. Large feature — only pursue with a concrete user-facing benefit.

title: i18n: renderer localisation support layer (language-neutral, EN master)
body:
Add a language-neutral localisation **support layer** to the renderer, so any user-facing UI string can be translated. This issue is about the **infrastructure**, not the translations themselves — English stays the master/source of truth; concrete languages are added on top afterwards.

## Requirement (the support layer)

- A global `t('area.key', { placeholders })` available to every renderer script — no build step, no module system (matches our vanilla `<script>` setup).
- **English is the master.** Missing keys fall back to English, then to the key itself — the UI is never blank.
- Static HTML localised via `data-i18n` / `data-i18n-title` / `data-i18n-placeholder` / `data-i18n-aria` attributes, filled on load.
- Active language is a global setting (Settings UI). Changing it persists and re-renders every view in the new language.
- Extract the existing English UI strings (sidebar tabs/chrome, session filters, settings labels/dialogs/tooltips) into the dictionary, keyed. No behaviour change while only EN exists.
- Language-neutral by design: adding a language = adding one dictionary, no code changes.

## Rollout (separate follow-up issues, not this one)

Language order once the layer lands: **EN (master) → DE → FR**. Each added language is its own translation task on top of this support layer.

## Work organisation

Large, cross-cutting change (touches nearly every renderer file that emits text) → **own feature branch**.

Reference implementation for the layer: kreaddis `public/i18n.js` (~488 lines) — adapt the mechanism, ignore its specific fr strings. Upstream commit `6e4d127` (fr apostrophe fix) is only relevant once translations exist.

Plan/design + string-extraction strategy go in comments.

title: Hermes: availability probe only checks the executable
body:
The Hermes availability probe resolves the `hermes` executable and nothing else. The plan asked it to check the toolchain Hermes needs (Python/uv, Node, ripgrep, ffmpeg), while tolerating that Hermes bundles its own MinGit (so a missing *system* git must not fail the probe).

In practice the shipped installer brings its own venv, so the exe check has been sufficient so far. If a Hermes install turns out to be able to resolve its binary while still being unable to run, extend the probe (`hermes doctor` can self-report) and surface the reason in Settings, where the probe result is already displayed.

title: Hermes: no busy/idle signal when the DB is unreadable (no fallback)
body:
Hermes' busy/idle state is derived entirely from its `state.db` row (a running turn has no `ended_at`, and its last message timestamp keeps moving). This works and is the better signal — but there is **no fallback**: if the DB cannot be read, the session simply never reports a state, and the tab shows nothing at all.

Hermes has a documented degraded mode where it writes sessions as JSON files instead of the DB. In that mode a live Hermes tab has no busy/idle indication whatsoever.

Requirement: when the store yields no state for a live session, fall back to generic PTY activity (the signal every terminal tab already has), so a session is never permanently stateless.

title: Multi-LLM: leftover gaps against the plan and the mockups
body:
Small clauses from the multi-LLM plan that were never implemented. Collected so they are not lost; none of them blocks anything.

- **Codex title source**: the plan named `session_index.jsonl` (`thread_name`) as the preferred title source; the parser only uses the first real user prompt.
- **Unresolved `$VAR` at launch**: a profile whose referenced env var is not set is flagged when saving, but not when launching.
- **Launch picker cosmetics**: no "CLI backends" group label, and the default backend's row carries no `default` tag (both in the mockup).
- **Stats filter scope**: the backend filter scopes only the per-backend breakdown, not the per-model charts above it.
- **Projects admin**: no "Backends" column showing which backends a project has sessions from.
- **Scheduled runs**: still record no backend overlay (they are Claude-only by design, and their provenance defaults to Claude, so this is cosmetic).
- **Cold-scan timing**: the startup scan duration was measured once by hand; it is not instrumented, so a regression would go unnoticed.

title: Backend hot paths: avoidable per-event work (Hermes re-parse, Pi node probe, store walks)
body:
Found by the final full-system review. None of these is user-visible today; they are the parts that will hurt first when a store grows.

- **Hermes `liveState` re-parses the whole session per watcher flush**, including the 500-message text pull, just to derive busy/idle. It needs two columns, not the transcript.
- **Hermes' change marker** runs a correlated `MAX(messages.timestamp)` subquery per session on every WAL poll.
- **Pi's probe shells out to `node --version` synchronously** on the main process, refreshed every 15s via the availability cache, and `backends.list()` is on the scan path.
- **`liveRefFor` walks the entire store** on every watcher flush for each not-yet-claimed session (it is tried before `matchLiveSession`).
- **`session-backends.json` never re-marks entries as persisted after a restart**, so the FIFO cap can never evict them and the file grows without bound.

title: Backends: extract the shared file-store boilerplate (Codex and Pi duplicate it)
body:
Every file-mode backend reimplements the same ~60 lines: a recursive walk of the store into `{kind:'file'}` handles, `watchTargets()`, a birth-time `matchLiveSession`, a filename-suffix `liveRefFor`, and a PATH-walking `findExecutable`. Codex and Pi carry it verbatim; the next file backend would copy it a third time, and a fix to one copy would not reach the others — which is exactly the failure pattern that produced #148–#155.

Requirement: a shared helper (`backends/file-store.js`) that a file-mode backend composes, so a new one declares its store root, its filename shape and its parser, and gets discovery, watching and the identity lookups for free.

---

The documentation half of this issue is **done**: the contract is now described as the code actually implements it, in [`docs/specs/09-multi-llm.md`](../blob/main/docs/specs/09-multi-llm.md) (including the three identity hooks the old plan omitted, and without the `deriveState(events)` hook nothing calls). `docs/backend-formats.md` records what each backend really writes.

title: A session that moved into a worktree still follows its original folder
body:
Split out of #147 (which is otherwise done: real worktrees are first-class projects).

A session that started in the parent repo and later moved into a worktree (or the other way round) is still attributed to the folder it started in, not to the directory it currently runs in.

Why it was not fixed there: a Claude project folder is keyed on the directory it was created from, and the project is derived **once for the whole folder**. Deriving it from a session's *current* cwd made one moved session drag every sibling session in that folder along with it, depending on readdir order.

Requirement: attribute each session to the project of its current working directory, without changing where its siblings live. That needs a per-session project column (the helpers already exist and are tested — `sessionCwd()` / `extractCurrentCwdFromJsonl()` — they are just deliberately not used for grouping).
